Thank you for requesting our views on the above matter. 
You 
have been asked the following question: 
May employees of a County contract with the County 
Board of Supervisors to provide the County, as 
independent contractors in the future, those public 
services they are presently performing as county 
employees? 
I will confine my Qlscussion to the possible application of 
the Poli tical Reform Act to this question. 
First, there is nothing in the Political Reform Act which 
would prohibit such a contract. 
As public officials, the 
employees are clearly permitted under the Act to negotiate 
contracts for their future services to the County. 
2 Cal. Adm. 
Code Section 18700 (d) I 
(f) (2) • 
However, although the employees may change their status to 
that of independent contractors, they will probably still be 
subject to the provisions of the Political Reform Act. 
They 
will have to be designated in the County's conflict of interest 
code as consultants if they meet the criteria of 2 Cal. Adm. 
Code Section 18700 (a) (2) I 
provided they make or participate in 
governmental decisions as defined by 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 
18700 (b) or (c). 
Government Code Sections 82019, 87302 . .1/ 
II All references are to the Government Code unless 
otherwise noted. 


Ronald 11. t'Jeiskopf 
March 29, 1983 
Page Two 
If they are consultants within the definition of 2 Cal. Adm. 
Code Section 18700 (a) (2), they will also be subj ect to the 
disqualification provisions of Section 87100, since the term 
"public official" in~ludes consultants. 
Section 82048. 
Finally, consultants within the meaning of the Act and 
regulations are covered by Sections 87400-87405 after they 
terminate their services to the government agency. 
Thus, if the 
employees change their status to that of independent contractors, 
and then later terminate that relationship with the County, they 
will be prohibited from undertaking private employment relating 
to certain proceedings in which they participated as consultants. 
I hope this has been of assistance to you. 
If any of these 
employees wants specific advice as to his or her individual 
duties under the Political Reform Act, he or she should request 
such advice from the Commission pursuant to Section 83114. 
If 
you have any fUTther questions, I will be happy to discuss them 
wi th you. 
Very truly yours,

sections.question: May employees of a County contract with the County Board of Supervisors to provide the County, as independent contractors in the future, those public services they are presently performing as county employees?
sections.conclusion: First, there is nothing in the Political Reform Act which would prohibit such a contract. As public officials, the employees are clearly permitted under the Act to negotiate contracts for their future services to the County. However, although the employees may change their status to that of independent contractors, they will probably still be subject to the provisions of the Political Reform Act. They will have to be designated in the County's conflict of interest code as consultants if they meet the criteria and provided they make or participate in governmental decisions. If they are consultants within the definition, they will also be subject to the disqualification provisions of Section 87100. Finally, consultants are covered by Sections 87400-87405 after they terminate their services to the government agency.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether county employees may contract with their County Board of Supervisors to provide public services as independent contractors rather than as employees.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The Political Reform Act does not prohibit such contracts, and employees may negotiate for independent contractor status. However, they will likely remain subject to the Act as designated consultants, including disqualification provisions and post-termination restrictions on private employment related to prior governmental decisions.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains both the incoming request letter from Deputy Attorney General Weiskopf and the FPPC response letter from Barbara Milman. The response letter clearly delineates the question and conclusion sections. All statutory references are to California Government Code and Administrative Code.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: May employees of a County contract with the County Board of Supervisors to provide the County, as independent contractors in the future, those public services they are presently performing as county employees?

CONCLUSION: First, there is nothing in the Political Reform Act which would prohibit such a contract. As public officials, the employees are clearly permitted under the Act to negotiate contracts for their future services to the County. However, although the employees may change their status to that of independent contractors, they will probably still be subject to the provisions of the Political Reform Act. They will have to be designated in the County's conflict of interest code as consultants if they meet the criteria and provided they make or participate in governmental decisions. If they are consultants within the definition, they will also be subject to the disqualification provisions of Section 87100. Finally, consultants are covered by Sections 87400-87405 after they terminate their services to the government agency.

embedding.summary: FPPC advice letter responding to a Deputy Attorney General's inquiry about whether county employees can contract with their County Board of Supervisors to perform as independent contractors the same public services they currently provide as employees. The FPPC concludes that while such contracts are permitted, the individuals will likely remain subject to Political Reform Act requirements as designated consultants.
